coredata.dc:title: An innovative strategy for construction of pH-responsive supramolecular hydrogel from graphene quantum dots clusters toward integration of detection and removal of uranium
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Jie
Zhou, Xiaoyuan
Huang, Yalin
Chen, Bo
Chen, Shanyong
Jin, Yongdong
Yang, Yuchuan
Pan, Ning
Xu, Chao
Chen, Jing
Xia, Chuanqin
coredata.dc:description: A novel supramolecular hydrogel (GQDs-GSH), generated from self-assembly between the reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and in-situ formed graphene quantum dots (GQDs), was fabricated by using one-step hydrothermal route. In the hydrogel, the GQDs clusters that produced by the aggregation of GQDs acted as the good cross-linkers, pillars and surface stabilizers to bind RGO building blocks via multiple hydrogen bonding to construct interconnected 3D porous network. Otherwise, the as-prepared hydrogel exhibited special pH-responsive on-off switchable fluorescence behavior, which means that the reversible process referred to stripping and reloading of GQDs in the hydrogel was regulated by the pH stimulation. Sequentially, GQDs-GSH was applied for U(VI) detection with a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.999) and high selectivity, followed by effective U(VI) removal and feasible GQDs recovery from aqueous solution. These exciting findings reveal good promises of GQDs for the preparation of 3D architectures and provide a potential platform for integrated detection and treatment of pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Graphene oxide
Hydrogel
pH-responsive
Uranium
Detection and removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection and removal of U(VI) and recovery of GQDs
The integration of detection and treatment of uranium and GQDs recovery by GQDs-GSH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: International Collaboration Project of Science and Technology Program of Sichuan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11475120, 11575122, 11775153, U1732107 ) and the International Collaboration Project of Science and Technology Program of Sichuan Province, China (Project No. 2017HH0056 ). We also thank the Testing Center, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University for the technical support. We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for the characterization of materials.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11475120, 11575122, 11775153, U1732107) and the International Collaboration Project of Science and Technology Program of Sichuan Province, China (Project No. 2017HH0056). We also thank the Testing Center, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University for the technical support. We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for the characterization of materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) Top view and (c) side view SEM images of GQDs-GSH; (d) TEM image of pristine GQDs solution; (e) TEM image of GQDs-GSH; (f) HRTEM image of GQDs with the lattice parameters of 0.20 nm in GQDs-GSH.
(a) Pore size distribution curve of GQDs-GSH; (b) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of GQDs-GSH; (c) Size distribution of GQDs clusters.
(a) High-resolution C1s and (b) N1s XPS spectra of the GQDs-GSH; (c) FT-IR spectra of GQDs-GSH GQDs; (d) TGA curves of GQDs-GSH and GH in N2 atmosphere.

(a) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of solutions after immersing GQDs-GSH in the aqueous solutions with pH value in the range of 1.0–13.0; (b) the effect of time on the fluorescence intensity of solutions after immersing GQDs-GSH into the aqueous solution at pH = 13.0; (c) photograph of immersing GQDs-GSH into the aqueous solution at pH = 13.0 for a few seconds (under illumination of 365 nm UV light); (d) SEM image of GQDs-GSH after immersing into the aqueous solution at pH = 13.0 for 240 min.

(a) The effect of time on the fluorescence intensity of solutions after mixing GQDs solution with RGO framework; (b) fluorescence intensity of solution upon the cyclic switching of GQDs-GSH under alternating conditions of pH = 13.0 and pH = 1.0.
Schematic diagram of reversibility of GQDs-GSH by pH stimulus.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs in the presence of different concentrations of U(VI) (0–1.26 mM). The inset is the linear relationship between fluorescence quenching ratio and log U(VI) concentration (logC U(VI)). (b) The influence of different metal ions on the fluorescence of GQDs. Cmetal ion = 0.5 mM, pH = 4.0. (c) Effect of U(VI) concentration on the adsorption of U(VI) onto RGO framework, Cu = 0–1.16 mM, pH = 4.0, m/V = 1 g L-1, t = 24 h, T = 298 K; (d) The effect of contact time on fluorescence intensity of solution with and without RGO framework after U(VI) detection.
Comparison of U(VI) detection and removal performance of GQDs-GSH and other reported materials.

head.ce:title: An innovative strategy for construction of pH-responsive supramolecular hydrogel from graphene quantum dots clusters toward integration of detection and removal of uranium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nuclear Physics and Chemistry, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621900, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nuclear Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fundamental Science on Nuclear Wastes and Environmental Safety Laboratory, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fundamental Science on Nuclear Wastes and Environmental Safety Laboratory
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fundamental Science on Nuclear Wastes and Environmental Safety Laboratory, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang 621010, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author.at: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel supramolecular hydrogel was easily synthesized from the self-assembly between the reduced graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots for integration of detection and treatment of uranium.

A novel supramolecular hydrogel (GQDs-GSH), generated from self-assembly between the reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and in-situ formed graphene quantum dots (GQDs), was fabricated by using one-step hydrothermal route. In the hydrogel, the GQDs clusters that produced by the aggregation of GQDs acted as the good cross-linkers, pillars and surface stabilizers to bind RGO building blocks via multiple hydrogen bonding to construct interconnected 3D porous network. Otherwise, the as-prepared hydrogel exhibited special pH-responsive on-off switchable fluorescence behavior, which means that the reversible process referred to stripping and reloading of GQDs in the hydrogel was regulated by the pH stimulation. Sequentially, GQDs-GSH was applied for U(VI) detection with a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.999) and high selectivity, followed by effective U(VI) removal and feasible GQDs recovery from aqueous solution. These exciting findings reveal good promises of GQDs for the preparation of 3D architectures and provide a potential platform for integrated detection and treatment of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Uranium is regarded as one of the fundamental nuclear fueles, but its inherent long half-life and carcinogenicity poses a serious threat to human health while uranium-containing acid wastewater is divulged into natural circumstances. Therefore, it is necessary to develop tactics that enable simultaneous detection and removal of uranium (U(VI)) for the sustainable development of the nuclear industry and environmental conservation [1]. Recently, researchers have developed various materials for detection and adsorption of U(VI), such as metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [2], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [3] and composites [4]. However, the low stability or poor U(VI) selectivity of limited reported materials hindered their application.

Hydrogels are functional polymer materials with significant three-dimensional network structure and are widely used in energy storage [5], biomedicine [6] and wastewater treatment [7]. In particular, stimuli-responsive supramolecular hydrogels are versatile and can be developed as “smart” materials for numerous applications in catalysis [8], drug/cell delivery [9] and metal ion detection [10] since the architectures of the hydrogels are responsive to pH [11], temperature [12] or irradiation [13] stimulus. For example, Kureha et al. developed rotaxane cross-linked hydrogels that exhibited a decoupled thermo- and pH-responsive volume transition for promising drug delivery [11]. Wu et al. prepared a NIR light-responsive hydrogel composed of peptide hydrogel and PEGylated NbSe2 for controlled release of cargos [14]. Huang et al. designed and synthesized a target-responsive smart hydrogel for rapid, portable, sensitive detection of U(VI) [10]. Recently, due to oxygen-rich functional groups that can be acted as the active assembly sites and the unique bulk layer features, graphene oxide (GO) or reduced graphene oxide (RGO) has been widely used as a precursor for the construction of graphene-based supramolecular hydrogels (GSH). In general, GSH exhibits outstanding high surface area, tough mechanical strength and high flexibility, which makes it popular [15]. Therefore, various GSH were synthesized from self-assembly of pure graphene sheets [16] or employing graphene as the building block for the assembly of DNA [17], chitosan [18], metal ions [19] or polymers [20] into macroscopic framework through π-π stacking, metal coordination, hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions [21,22]. Among aforementioned interfacial interactions, hydrogen bonding is a more versatile motif and has been designed to promote self-assembly flexibility for imparting interesting self-healable and stimuli-responsive properties into hydrogel [23]. However, up to now, there are few reports on adsorption of uranium by using GSH. Therefore, great efforts are needed to develop new GSH materials to make full use of the advantages of graphene in metal ions removal [24].

In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as appealing fluorescence sensors to detect U(VI) because of their excellent photoluminescence properties, chemical stability and good solubility [25]. It is noted that the U(VI) ions after detecting may be difficult to remove by ordinary adsorbents due to the strong binding between U(VI) and GQDs. However, up to now, no research focuses on the removal of uranium detected by GQDs. On the other hand, due to unique quantum confinement and edge effects, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) play significant roles in the fabrication of GQDs/graphene hydrogel composites for the electrocatalysis [26] and supercapacitor [27] applications. However, up to now, the reported synthesis methods of these composites usually required several steps with tedious and complicated operations, which were uneconomic and even environment-hazardous [28]. Furthermore, in obtained composites, GQDs were only attached to the surfaces of graphene and did not take part in the construction of final hydrogel network. As a result, the hydrogel structure is not affected by the introduction of GQDs [29,30]. In fact, due to the presence of abundant active groups and graphene-like structures, GQDs may be used as a significant component material to build a strong 3D GSH network. Therefore, it is possible to develop strategies to design and construct special GSH based on GQDs for the detcetion and timely removal of U(VI).

Herein, we proposed a facile one-step hydrothermal route to synthesize a novel graphene quantum dots-graphene supramolecular hydrogel (GQDs-GSH) in success. The self-assembly between RGO and GQDs by hydrogen bonding in the hydrogel has been demonstrated, in which GQDs clusters acted as both of the cross-linker, pillar and stabilizer to bind with the RGO sheets to form the interconnected porous 3D network. Interestingly, the obtained hydrogel exhibited unique pH-responsive properties, relying on the flexible hydrogen bonding network that was susceptible to pH stimulus. Moreover, the most significant innovation of this article is that the hydrogel combined the fluorescence sensing capability of GQDs with the superior adsorption performance of graphene, which was used as a smart platform for integrated detection and removal of U(VI) as well as recovery of GQDs.

GO powder was prepared by a modified Hummers' method [31]. Anhydrous citric acid (CA), urea, ammonium hydroxide (25–28%), NaOH and HCl were purchased from Chengdu Forest Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). All reagents were of analytical grade (AR) and used as received without further purification. Deionized water (18.25 MΩ∙cm) was used in all the experiments. Section S1 in the Supplementary Material provides synthesis details of GQDs and graphene hydrogel (GH) used in this study.

Thirty mg of GO and 115 mg of anhydrous citric acid were dispersed/dissolved in 10 mL of deionized (DI) water upon sonication for 30 min. Subsequently, ammonium hydroxide (25–28 vol%) was used to adjust the pH to ∼ 3.0 under vigorous stirring and the obtained suspension was sealed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 48 h. Then naturally cooling to room temperature, the as-prepared hydrogel was soaked in deionized water with pH 3.0 for 24 h to remove impurities and then freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and removal of U(VI) and recovery of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh GQDs-GSH was weighed and immersed into 10 mL of the aqueous solutions of NaOH (pH = 13.0) for 4 h, and then the solution was collected by filtration and adjusted to pH 4.0 by using HNO3 (w% = 65–68%), which used as GQDs stock solution. The retained cylindrical solids (RGO framework) were collected and washed with deionized water to neutral, then freeze-dried and weighed for next experiments.

The U(VI) detection experiment was carried out by mixing 100 μL of GQDs solution and 4.9 mL of U(VI) solutions with different concentrations (0, 0.0021, 0.0042, 0.0084, 0.021, 0.042, 0.084, 0.21, 0.34, 0.63, 1.26 mM) at pH = 4.0 for 30 min, and the fluorescence intensity of the different test solutions was recorded with the excitation wavelength fixed at 330 nm. The fluorescence quenching ratio ((F0-F)/F0) was used to evaluate the quenching ability of GQDs caused by U(VI) ions, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the GQD solution in the absence and presence of U(VI) ions, respectively. This study was aim at studying the detection of U(VI) in acid nuclear waste. On the one hand, U(VI) in the detection system (Cu = 0–1.26 mM) would precipitate as UO2(OH)2·H2O or schoepite when the pH of the solution exceeds 4.0 [32]. On the other hand, the interaction between U(VI) and the reported GQDs increased with the increasing of pH value in acid nuclear waste [3,25]. Therefore, 4.0 was used as the best pH for U(VI) detection. Furthermore, according to species distribution simulation for U(VI) hydrolysis, uranyl ion (UO2 2+) was the dominate species for detection and adsorption at pH 4.0 [32,33].

The removal of U(VI): after U(VI) detections, aforementioned test solutions were added 5.0 mg of RGO framework and the mixture was left to stand for 24 h at room temperature. After isolating the solid by centrifugation, the concentration of the U(VI) in supernatant was analyzed by ICP-OES. The amount of adsorbed U(VI) on RGO framework (qe , mg/g) and the removal efficiency (E, %) were calculated by the Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), respectively:
(1)
q e
=
C 0
-
C e
V
m
(2)
E =
C 0
-
C e
C 0
× 100 %
where C0 (mg/L) and Ce (mg/L) are the concentration of U(VI) before and after adsorption, V (L) and m (g) are the volume of solution and adsorbent dosage, respectively.

The recovery of GQDs: the recovery experiments of GQDs were performed by adjusting the test solution to pH 1.0 and then RGO framework was added. Another same mixed solution without RGO framework as a control. The fluorescence intensity of the two mixed solutions at different contact times was recorded with the excitation wavelength fixed at 330 nm.

Field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) combined with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) operated a 5–18 kV accelerating voltage. TEM was performed using a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and the pore size distribution were measured by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) equation with instrument (micromeritics, TriStar II 3020). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 670 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from a DX-1000 system with Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.540598 Å) operating at a scan rate of 0.06° s−1 in the 2θ range of 5-70° (Dandong, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed on an Axis Ultra DLD XPS spectrometer using monochromatic Al Kα radiation (hν = 15 kV) (Kratos Analytical, UK). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out by a DTG-60(H) thermogravimetric analyzer at a heating rate of 15 °C min−1 in N2 atmosphere (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F96pro fluorescence spectrophotometer. The concentration of metal ions in aqueous solutions was measured on an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Optima 8000, PerkinElmer, USA). The particle size was determined by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instrument, Worcs, UK) equipped with 4 mW He-Ne Laser.

The GQDs-GSH was synthesized through mixing GO, CA and NH3·H2O, and following by a one-step hydrothermal process, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The low-cost CA was chosen as the carbon source of the GQDs, NH3·H2O acted as the catalyst for the dehydration of CA in the growth of the GQDs and used for N doping of GQDs. In the hydrothermal process, RGO (also called graphene) sheets were obtained and used as the building blocks for the assembly of in situ generated GQDs by the multiple hydrogen bonding interactions to construct bulky hydrogel network. Furthermore, the successful preparation of the hydrogels was also benefit from a variety of well-chosen reaction conditions (Supplementary Material). All results revealed that the one-step synthesis method that we designed are simple and superior, here the GQDs plays a crucial role in determining the final hydrogel structure.

The interior microstructures of as-prepared GQDs-GSH were examined by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, GQDs-GSH exhibited an interconnected 3D network with obvious porosity. The pore diameters are varied from sub-micrometer to a few micrometers and the pore walls were promiscuous, but no broken graphene fragment was found (Fig. 2b), which indicated that RGO sheets were fully utilized for the formation of the hydrogel network due to the partial coalescing of flexible RGO sheets and strong interfacial interaction between adjacent RGO nanosheets and GQDs. Side view SEM image of GQDs-GSH (Fig. 2c) showed some huge gaps, confirming that the GQDs penetrated into the hydrogel to prevent agglomeration of the RGO sheets. The porous structure of the GQDs-GSH was also demonstrated by the N2 adsorption-desorption measurement. As shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b, BJH calculations disclosed a wide distribution of pores size lied in the range of 1.7–300 nm with an average pore diameter of 9.46 nm, and the pore volume was calculated about 0.42 cm3 g−1. The BET surface area reached up to 149 m2 g−1 due to the existence of interconnected micro-, meso-, and macro-pores. The diverse pore size, large pore volume and high surface area provide a promising utilization of GQDs-GSH in catalysis and water purification (as a sorbent).

TEM images of GQDs-GSH showed that GQDs were decorated on the surface of graphene and exhibited a good crystalline structure with the in-plane lattice spacing of 0.20 nm which corresponded to the [110] crystallographic direction of graphite (Fig. 2e and 2f). Interestingly, several GQDs clusters with the sizes of dozens of nanometers to hundreds of nanometers were also observed in hydrogel (Fig. 2e), which demonstrated that the GQDs were synthesized and nonuniformly decorated on graphene sheets of the GQDs-GSH with spontaneous agglomeration. The result was first discovered and significantly different from the previously reported studies which showed that GQDs were mono-dispersed on surface of graphene and at least two orders of magnitude smaller than graphene sheet in GQDs/graphene hydrogel composites [29,30]. Similar GQDs clusters were also found in TEM image of pristine GQDs solution (Fig. 2d), and no single GQD was observed. Moreover, the size distribution of GQDs clusters was detected by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 3c). The result showed that the diameters of GQDs clusters were in the wide range of 20–1300 nm and the average diameter was ca. 290.1 nm, which was consistent with TEM observations. Those findings indicated strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding interaction of GQDs. All above results pointed out that the GQDs clusters might take part in the construction of hydrogel network due to enlarged size and rich active sites.

The composition and structure of as-prepared GQDs-GSH were examined by XRD, Raman, XPS and FT-IR analyses. The XRD patterns of GQDs-GSH showed two broad diffraction peaks that appeared at 2θ values of 24.5 and 42.7° (Fig. S2a), which were close to the typical (002) diffraction peak of graphite and (111) diffraction peak of diamond, respectively. Those results confirmed that GO was reduced by hydrothermal treatment and the obtained GQDs-GSH was amorphous [34]. In the Raman spectrum of GQDs-GSH (Fig. S2b), two peaks were found around 1348 and 1600 cm−1, corresponding to the well-documented D band (the presence of sp3 defects) and G band (the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon) of graphite, respectively. ID/IG ratio was calculated to be about 1.7, revealing disorder stack and a large number of defects of graphene which owing to the presence of abundant functional groups.

The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S4a) showed that the GQDs-GSH was mainly composed of carbon (285.8 eV), nitrogen (399.6 eV) and oxygen (531.8 eV) elements. In the high resolution C1s spectrum of the GQDs-GSH (Fig. 4 a), four main peaks located at 284.6, 285.7, 286.8 and 290.1 eV were assigned to C-C, C-N, C-O and C = O groups, respectively. The N1s spectrum of the GQDs-GSH (Fig. 4b) presented three peaks which were ascribed to pyridinic-N (399.3 eV), pyrrolic-N (400.0 eV) and graphitic-N (401.3 eV). These oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups were further confirmed by the FT-IR analysis (Fig. 4c), in which the peaks at 3432, 2922, 1719, 1566 and 1179 cm−1 were attributed to O-H, C-H, C = O, C-N and C-OH vibrations, respectively. These results depicted that a great deal of hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine and imino functional groups existed on the surface of GQDs-GSH. Furthermore, similar elements composition and functional groups were also found in the UV–vis, PL, FT-IR (Fig. S3a-c) and XPS (Fig. S4a-d) analyses of GQDs. It revealed that in situ formed GQDs were introduced into graphene matrix during hydrothermal treatment, which is also supported by TEM observations.

TGA was carried out to study the thermal nature of GQDs-GSH and graphene hydrogel (GH), and the results are shown in Fig. 4d. The weight loss of GQDs-GSH and GH below 110 °C was about 9.5% and 8.8%, respectively, which was attributed to the adsorbed water. A higher loss of GQDs-GSH than GH indicated that GQDs-GSH encapsulated more water due to the introduction of GQDs. In the range of 110–600 °C, the weight loss of GH was 15.7%, corresponding to the decomposition of oxygenated groups. For GQDs-GSH, the weight loss in the range of 110–300 °C was 7.5% and the most significant weight loss in the range of 300–600 °C was 11.8%, assigned to the loss of the oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups. Two weight loss stages in the TGA cueve of GQDs-GSH demonstrated that different interactions take place between the RGO matrix and the GQDs. In the GQDs-GSH, the functional groups of the RGO and the GQDs that are not involved in bonding lost in the range of 110–300 °C, while the other groups that participated in cross-linking by hydrogen bonding were destroyed at a higher temperature (300–600 °C). Moreover, the residual weight of GQDs-GSH and GH at 600 °C was 71.2% and 75.5%, respectively, which revealed that GQDs-GSH and GH both displayed excellent thermal stability, but more stable covalent attachment happened in GH.

The self-assembly mechanism and the roles of GQDs in the hydrogel were investigated in detail. The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-GSH (Fig. 4c) showed that most of peaks of functional groups were significantly weakened (O-H at 3419 cm−1, C-O at 1228 cm−1, C-N at 1123 cm−1) or even disappeared (N-H at 3155 cm−1 and C = O at 1713 cm−1) as compared to that of GQDs. Furthermore, the typical peak belonging to O-H deformation vibration (1400 cm−1) was disappeared. These representative features revealed that the presence of strong hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine as well as imino functional groups of GQDs and polar groups of RGO [35]. Thus hydrogen bonding as the key force drives GQDs to bind RGO sheets to produce an interconnected hydrogel network during the hydrothermal progress.

In order to further disclose the roles of GQDs in the self-assembly, we have explored the effect of urea on GQDs-GSH structure since urea is known to disrupt original hydrogen bonding [36]. When GQDs-GSH was immersed in a 30% (wt/vol) solution of urea (pH = 8.45) for 30 min, the solution became yellow and exhibited bright blue luminescence under the illumination of UV beam (365 nm) (Fig. S5a, left). However, when GQDs-GSH was simply immersed in deionized water (pH = 8.45), no any luminescence was observed (Fig. S5a, right) and the solution was still colorless after 30 min. Fluorescence spectra of the yellow solution showed a strong emission peak while a negligible emission was recorded for the colorless solution (Fig. S5b). These results indicated that a large number of GQDs were stripped from the hydrogel into solution by the urea treatment. What's more, the SEM images the GQDs-GSH soaked in the urea solution (Fig S6a and S6b) showed that most of the pores disappeared and the RGO sheets became thick and smooth, indicating that the 3D continuous network of the hydrogel collapsed and transformed into a 2D structure due to the absence of GQDs barriers. These findings suggested that the hydrogen bonding interaction between GQDs and RGO sheets was broken because urea molecules could donate richer N-H bonds to oxygen atoms of RGO sheets than GQDs [36,37]. Subsequently, GQDs was expelled into the solution. The FT-IR spectrum of the hydrogel was similar to that of urea (Fig. S6c), suggesting that plenty of urea molecules were captured by graphene sheets. Moreover, an obvious downshift of all peaks for hydrogel was observed, resulting from the existence of new hydrogen bonding between graphene and urea. These results proved that GQDs clusters acted as both effective cross-linkers and pillars of graphene building blocks in the hydrogel.

Furthermore, in-situ formed GQDs were found to be the surface stabilizer of the RGO sheets. As shown in Fig. S7, graphene hydrogel (GH) showed much smaller size (Fig. S7a) and more significant convolution and denser stack of graphene sheets (Fig. S7b and S7c) compared to GQDs-GSH, which should be attributed to different assembly mechanisms for the two hydrogels. The GH was driven by strong π-π stacking interactions [16], thus graphene sheets in GH were compact to form a tight architecture. As a result, limited growth of 3D network happened in GH hydrogel because of the underutilization of surface area and functional groups. For GQDs-GSH, the doping of in-situ formed GQDs through the intense hydrogen bonding between GQDs and RGO weakened the π-π stacking interactions and helped to prevented the spontaneous agglomeration of graphene [38], which guaranteed the accessibility and availability of internal active sites of hydrogel to form a bulky network. All above results demonstrated that GQDs could stabilize grephene surface, which is benefit for further self-assembly process.

In order to study the special pH-responsive behaviors of GQDs-GSH, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of supernatants was investigated by immersing GQDs-GSH into the aqueous solutions over the wide range of pH 1.0–13.0. As shown in Fig. 5 a, when the pH value of the solution was below 3.0, negligible fluorescence intensity was detected, indicating that no GQD was detached from the hydrogel into the solution. In the range of pH 3.0–13.0, increasing fluorescence intensity for the corresponding solutions were observed, suggesting increasing release of GQDs from the hydrogels into the solutions with increasing pH. When pH reached up to 13.0, the rapid escape of GQDs from the hydrogel into solution was accomplished within 120 min (Fig. 5b) and was visible to the naked eye under the illumination using 365 nm UV beam (Fig. 5c). It also means the separation of quantum dots from graphene in the hydrogel at pH = 13.0. Above observations suggested an interesting mechanism for the assembly and disassembly of the hydrogel depended on different pH. At low pH, protonated functional groups were favor of interacting with each other via the hydrogen bonding, resulting in stable connection. At high pH, the functional groups of the hydrogel exacerbated the deprotonation, following by the strong electrostatic repulsion to destroy the hydrogen bonding of between RGO and GQDs [39]. In consequence, GQDs were stripped from hydrogel network, at the same time, some local structure collapses of the residual hydrogel solid (named RGO framework) were inevitable due to the absence of GQDs (Fig. 5d). It noted that the content of GQDs in the hydrogel was calculated to be 18.5% by weight method, indicating that a large number of quantum dots participated in the construction of the hydrogel network.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The integration of detection and treatment of uranium and GQDs recovery by GQDs-GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Based on unique structures, properties and self-assembly mechanism, GQDs-GSH will promise various applications such as molecular recognition, sensor and drug delivery. Here, the separated GQDs that stripped from hydrogel were exploited for the detection of U(VI). As shown in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence intensity clearly decreased with the increasing concentration of U(VI), indicating uranyl ions could quench the fluorescence of GQDs by coordination interaction between uranyl ions and the oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups of GQDs [40]. What's more, a good linear relationship was found between fluorescence intensity and logC U(VI) in the concentration range of 0 to 1.26 mM with the R2 up to 0.999. It should be noted that the R2 was superior to that of reported fluorimetric materials (as shown in Table 1 ). Furthermore, the fluorescence of GQDs was significantly quenched by U(VI) but was negligibly affected by a series of metal ions including Cs+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr2+ (Fig. 8b), indicating that GQDs exhibited predominant selectivity for U(VI) due to the stronger coordination ability and electrostatic attraction (the value of effective charge density of uranium in uranyl is 3.2+ ) of uranyl ion compared with other competing metal ions [4,31,40]. Therefore, the developed method in this study was reliable for monitoring U(VI) by using GQDs as sensing probes.

More interestingly, RGO framework was used to eliminate the U(VI) elements from test solution (Cu = 0–1.16 mM) after U(VI) detection. As can be seen from Fig. 8c, the adsorption coefficients of U(VI) on the RGO framework were 100% under low U(VI) concentration (Cu ≤ 0.3 mM), indicating complete uranium recovery. In the U(VI) concentration region of 0.3–1.16 mM, the adsorption efficient of U(VI) by RGO framework decreased but the adsorption capacity increased with the increasing U(VI) concentration, and the maximum adsorption capacity was achieved about 120.6 mg g−1. These results showed the effective removal of U(VI) from test solutions by RGO framework due to the strong complexation between U(VI) and the functional groups of graphene sheets. It should be noted that this study is the first case focused on the timely removal of U(VI) after detection. Thus the U(VI) adsorption performance of RGO is incomparable with that of the reported materials (in a single uranium system) due to the interference of GQDs (Table 1).

In addition, RGO framework was also used to capture the GQDs from test solution under acidic conditions (pH = 1.0) after U(VI) detection. As shown in Fig. 8d, the fluorescence intensity of the test solution mixed with RGO framework for 48 h decreased to a negligible level, while the fluorescence intensity of the test solution without RGO framework remained constant with the increase of contact time. The results demonstrated that the GQDs could be recovered from test solution into RGO framework for reuse after U(VI) detection, and while uranium was left in solution. Therefore, GQDs-GSH is a pathbreaking and versatile material for integrated application in detection and elimination of U(VI) as well as GQDs recovery.

In summary, a supramolecular hydrogel (GQDs-GSH) was successfully fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal method. The GQDs-GSH was the first case of using GQDs clusters as cross-linkers to binding RGO for constructing interconnected 3D porous network. Thanks to the pH-dependent hydrogen bonding between oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups, as-prepared GQDs-GSH exhibited excellent reversible assembly of GQDs and RGO in response to pH stimulus. Based on the special pH-responsive properties, GQDs-GSH was used for effective detection and removal of U(VI) as well as recovery of GQDs. Therefore, this study provides an novel strategy for the design of GQDs-based hydrogel and the integration of analysis and treatment of radionuclides.

Jie Ding: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xiaoyuan Zhou: Visualization. Yalin Huang: Software. Bo Chen: Investigation. Shanyong Chen: Writing – review & editing. Yongdong Jin: Resources, Visualization. Yuchuan Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Ning Pan: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Chao Xu: Conceptualization, Resources. Jing Chen: Conceptualization, Resources. Chuanqin Xia: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11475120, 11575122, 11775153, U1732107) and the International Collaboration Project of Science and Technology Program of Sichuan Province, China (Project No. 2017HH0056). We also thank the Testing Center, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University for the technical support. We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for the characterization of materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.152492.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
